title: Cross-check reading deck facts and references against walt-market-research
description:
Cross-check all facts, statistics, and references used in the reading deck Problem slide (and References slide) against existing data in the walt-market-research repo. Verify accuracy, flag discrepancies, and ensure all cited sources are substantiated.

## Facts and figures to verify

### 1. "200–250M Europeans use Apple Pay or Google Wallet for in-store purchases" (Ref 1)
- **How derived**: Flagship Advisory Partners (2023) reported 44% of Europeans use mobile wallets. With ~450M EU/EEA internet users, that gives ~200M total mobile wallet users. Mordor Intelligence (2025) corroborates with ~300M contactless payment users in Europe (though this includes physical card contactless). Apple Pay dominates (~60-63% in-store share in UK/France per Chargeflow), Google Wallet trails significantly.
- **Cited sources**: Flagship Advisory Partners "6 Years After the EU Launch of Apple Pay" (2023); Mordor Intelligence "Europe Mobile Payments Market" (2025)
- **Check against**: walt-market-research/research/apple-pay-google-wallet-statistics.md, walt-market-research/research/european-market-sizing.md

### 2. "€500B+ in mobile wallet payments at European point-of-sale terminals (2024)" (Ref 2)
- **How derived**: ECB SPACE 2024 reports mobile device payments = 7% of POS payment value in the euro area. If total euro area POS value ~€5.1T (derived from ECB card payments €2.3T = 45% of total POS), then 7% = ~€357B for euro area. Scaling to include UK and non-euro EEA gives ~€450-550B. Rounded to €500B+.
- **Alternative calculation**: Worldpay GPR 2025 says digital wallets = 14% of European POS value, giving ~€714B, but this broader definition includes PayPal/A2A wallets, not just NFC phone payments.
- **Cited sources**: ECB SPACE 2024; ECB Payment Statistics H2 2024; Mordor Intelligence
- **Check against**: walt-market-research/research/eu-tap-to-pay-landscape.md (Worldpay 14% POS share data)

### 3. "<0.4% combined market share for everyone else" (Ref 3)
- **Cited sources**: CoinLaw "Apple Pay vs Google Pay Statistics" (2026); StatCounter
- **Check against**: walt-market-research/research/apple-pay-google-wallet-statistics.md, walt-market-research/research/competitive-landscape.md

### 4. "Apple Pay charges European banks ~0.05% per transaction" (Ref 4)
- **How derived**: Apple does not publicly disclose European fee rates. The 0.05% estimate comes from: Swiss antitrust investigation (Finews) showing 0.05% for web/app transactions, 0.12% for standard credit; Russian data showing 0.05% debit / 0.12% credit; EU Interchange Fee Regulation 2015/751 caps (0.3% credit, 0.2% debit) constraining Apple's fee. The US rate of 0.15% is well-documented (DOJ complaint, WSJ). Existing walt-market-research already cites ~0.05% (eu-tap-to-pay-landscape.md line 71).
- **Decision**: Presented as "~0.05%" with tilde to indicate estimate. Should be framed as industry estimate, not Apple-confirmed.
- **Cited sources**: Swiss antitrust investigation via Finews; EU Interchange Fee Regulation 2015/751
- **Check against**: walt-market-research/research/eu-tap-to-pay-landscape.md (line 71)

### 5. "Extracts €300–500M from European transactions" (Ref 5)
- **Source**: Oliver Wyman (February 2025) estimated Apple Pay generates EUR 300-500M in fees across European markets where it handles ~35% of non-cash POS transactions.
- **Cited sources**: Oliver Wyman, European Apple Pay fee revenue estimate (February 2025)
- **Check against**: walt-market-research/research/eu-tap-to-pay-landscape.md

### 6. "Harvests every transaction for ad profiles and selling to 3rd parties" (Ref 6)
- **Sources**: DOJ v. Apple antitrust complaint (March 2024) describes Google's data practices by comparison; WSJ reporting on ad-profile monetization. Google's Store Sales Direct program (Bloomberg 2018) revealed Google paid Mastercard millions for access to 2B cardholders' transaction data.
- **Cited sources**: DOJ v. Apple antitrust complaint (March 2024); WSJ; Bloomberg/TechCrunch (2018 Google-Mastercard deal)
- **Check against**: walt-market-research/research/apple-pay-google-wallet-statistics.md (Section 3)

### 7. "Estimated €1B+ annually in ad value from European payment data" (Refs 7+8)
- **How derived**: Two-part calculation:
  - Ref 7 (per-user value): Alphabet 10-K shows EMEA revenue $102B (2024), 77% from ads = ~$78.6B EMEA ad revenue. Europe ~75-80% of EMEA = ~$59-63B (€54-58B). With ~400M European Google users = ~€140/year per user. Proton data valuation methodology and ad-tech industry benchmarks suggest payment/transaction data contributes 10-20% of targeting value = €15-30/user/year.
  - Ref 8 (European adoption): Google Wallet active users in Europe estimated 40-80M based on ~190M Android phones in Europe, country-level adoption surveys (UK 9.41%, Germany 2.75%), and PYMNTS tap-to-pay usage data.
  - Combined: 40-80M users × €15-30/user = €0.6-2.4B, mid-range ~€1-1.5B, conservatively stated as €1B+.
- **Supporting evidence**: Revolut targets £300M/year ad revenue from transaction data (Finance Magnates). PayPal building $1B+ ad business on transaction data (Payments Dive).
- **Cited sources**: Alphabet 10-K (2024); Proton data valuation; Statista country adoption; PYMNTS
- **Check against**: walt-market-research/research/b2b-whitelabel-opportunity.md (line 39, €15-30/user), walt-market-research/research/european-market-sizing.md

## Other decisions made in this session
- Changed global figure €8.3T to European POS-specific €500B+ (more relevant to Walt's tap-to-pay story)
- Apple Pay fee changed from 0.15% (US rate) to ~0.05% (European estimate)
- Removed Curve Pay from Problem slide; replaced with "the only alternative is physical cards or cash"
- Removed "No privacy-first NFC wallet exists" → "No legitimate tap-to-pay alternative exists for either iPhone or Android"

## Files to cross-reference in walt-market-research
- research/apple-pay-google-wallet-statistics.md
- research/european-market-sizing.md
- research/eu-tap-to-pay-landscape.md
- research/competitive-landscape.md
- research/b2b-whitelabel-opportunity.md
- research/privacy-tech-business-models.md
- research/product-marketing-context.md
close_reason: PR created: https://github.com/bittelc/walt-market-research/pull/2 — all 7 claims documented with verification status, action items, and methodology.

title: Update one-liner to 'European tap-to-pay' across website and pitch deck
description: Walt's strongest one-liner is 'European tap-to-pay'. Update the website and pitch deck to use this as the attention grabber in all the right places — hero sections, meta descriptions, headlines, slide titles, etc.

title: Clarify Linear aesthetic reference vs current warm/rounded style
description: Design context cites Linear as inspiration (precision, clarity). Linear uses dark/neutral backgrounds, precise spacing, color only for interactive elements. Current Walt site has warm gradients, 40px border-radius cards, peach-tinted sections — more friendly fintech than Linear. Question: Is the Linear reference about overall feel (clean, precise, minimal) or specific visual language (dark tones, sharp geometry, monochromatic)?
design:
# Linear Direction Experiment Plan

## Decision
Move closer to Linear's visual language: sharp geometry, neutral backgrounds, color only for interactive elements. Keep Walt's orange identity but use it with more restraint.

## Worktree
walt-website-linear-direction (branch: linear-direction)

## Changes

### 1. Tighten border radii (CSS variables in :root)
- `--radius-section: 40px` → `16px`
- `--radius-pill: 48px` → `100px` (keep pill shape for inputs/buttons — Linear does this too)
- `--radius-phone-small: 24px` → `20px` (match --radius-phone)
- `--radius-card: 12px` → `8px`
- `--radius-sm: 6px` → `4px`

Affected selectors (no changes needed, they use the variables):
- .hero-card (line 194) — uses --radius-section
- .section-card (line 479) — uses --radius-section
- .bottom-cta-card (line 959) — uses --radius-section
- .references (line 1369) — uses --radius-section
- .hero-input (line 278) — uses --radius-pill
- .hero-button (line 279) — uses --radius-pill
- .hero-secondary-button (line 331) — uses --radius-pill
- .pitch-form-input (line 299) — uses --radius-pill
- .deck-present-button (line 1787) — uses --radius-pill
- .pitch-download-button (line 1527) — uses --radius-pill

### 2. Neutralize section backgrounds
Replace warm orange-tinted section gradients with neutral gray:
- `--section-gradient-start: rgba(255, 72, 0, 0.06)` → `rgba(0, 0, 0, 0.02)`
- `--section-gradient-end: rgba(255, 72, 0, 0.12)` → `rgba(0, 0, 0, 0.04)`

Replace direct warm rgba backgrounds with neutral equivalents:
- .step-number (line 459): `rgba(255, 72, 0, 0.1)` → `rgba(0, 0, 0, 0.06)`
- .usp-icon (line 532): `rgba(255, 72, 0, 0.1)` → `rgba(0, 0, 0, 0.06)`
- .roadmap-current-marker (line 757): `rgba(255, 72, 0, 0.1)` → keep (functional indicator)
- .references (line 1367): `rgba(255, 72, 0, 0.05)` → `rgba(0, 0, 0, 0.02)`
- .ps-backdrop-glow::before (line 2244): `rgba(255, 72, 0, 0.04)` → `rgba(0, 0, 0, 0.03)`

Replace warm hex backgrounds:
- .deck-slide--warm (line 1873): `#fdf8f5` → `#f5f5f5`

Leave unchanged (functional, not decorative):
- .form-message.error (line 940) — error state, appropriate use of color
- .roadmap-current-marker (line 757) — active state indicator
- .roadmap-active-line mobile (line 1222) — active state indicator

### 3. Refine the hero card
The full-bleed orange gradient is the boldest design element. Two options:
- **Option A (recommended)**: Keep the orange gradient hero but tighten the radius. The hero is Walt's signature moment — orange is the brand, and restraining it everywhere else makes this one bold use more impactful.
- **Option B**: Replace with a subtle neutral card and use orange only for the CTA button.

Implement Option A — the hero stays orange, everything else gets neutral.
Same treatment for .bottom-cta-card (line 954) and .motivation-hero (line 1259) — these are intentional brand moments.

### 4. Reduce orange in decorative elements
- .deck-grid-item:hover box-shadow (line 1832): `rgba(255, 72, 0, 0.1)` → `rgba(0, 0, 0, 0.08)`
- .deck-tam-bar--som gradient (line 2089): keep — it's a data visualization, color is functional

### 5. Update design-context.md
Add explicit guidance under Aesthetic Direction:
- Clarify that Linear reference means adopting its precise geometry (tight radii, neutral backgrounds) while keeping Walt's orange for hero moments and CTAs
- Add specific radius and background guidance

## Scope
This is CSS-only. No HTML/JSX changes needed — all styling flows through CSS variables and classes.

## Verification
After changes, run `npm run build` to verify no build errors, then visually inspect in browser.
close_reason: Tightened border radii and removed NOW badge. Broader Linear-direction changes abandoned after review.

title: Multi-step signup: collect country and bank after email
description: After submitting email to waitlist, the form transitions in-place to optionally ask for country and bank. Email is saved to Resend immediately. Extra profile data sent as notification email. Subtle UX: same input box, fade animation, skip links.
close_reason: Discarded — Resend cannot store custom metadata on contacts, need a proper data store first

title: Evaluate orange color dominance vs 'sparingly' intent
description: The design context says color should be used sparingly — emphasis and CTAs, not decoration. Currently the hero card is a full-bleed orange gradient (~60% of viewport), section cards use orange-tinted gradients, and inner pages have solid orange hero banners. Question: Is this level of orange what's intended, or has it drifted beyond 'sparingly'?

title: Add reading deck to pitch website
description: Create a reading deck in addition to the current investor deck. The reading deck will have more words and likely more slides. Refactor the site to support both deck types.
design:
## Architecture

Extract shared presentation engine from pitch.js, keep slides co-located with their page files.

```
components/
  DeckLayout.js          ← NEW: shared grid/presentation engine
  Footer.js              ← ADD "reading-deck" variant
pages/
  pitch.js               ← REFACTOR: import DeckLayout, keep 9 slide components
  reading-deck.js        ← NEW: 11 slides with expanded content
styles/
  globals.css            ← ADD reading-deck text classes
public/
  reading-deck/          ← NEW: empty dir for future PDF/PPTX exports
```

## Step 1: Create components/DeckLayout.js

Extract from pages/pitch.js lines 627-891:
- SlideThumb component (ResizeObserver thumbnail scaler, SLIDE_REF_WIDTH = 1200)
- All state management (mode, currentSlide, navigation callbacks)
- Keyboard event handler (useEffect for arrows/space/escape)
- Full page JSX: deck-page wrapper, hero-section, deck-grid, deck-presentation overlay, Footer

Props: slides, head, heroTitle, heroDescription, downloads, footerVariant

## Step 2: Refactor pages/pitch.js

- Remove SlideThumb, SLIDE_REF_WIDTH, and the Pitch default export
- Keep all 9 slide components and SLIDES array untouched
- New default export: thin wrapper passing props to DeckLayout
- Investor deck must be pixel-identical after refactor

## Step 3: Update components/Footer.js

Add "reading-deck" variant with links: LinkedIn, Home, Pitch Deck, Motivation, Updates, Privacy.

## Step 4: Create pages/reading-deck.js

11 slides drawing content from walt-market-research/research/:

1. Title - Same as investor
2. The Problem - Specific privacy violations, €8.3T transaction value, 1.0-1.3B combined users
3. Solution - Expand B2C/B2B2C, on-device encryption, cross-platform DMA story
4. Why Now? - DMA detail (10-year Apple commitment), eIDAS 2.0, PSR/PSD3 FRAND, digital euro
5. Product Deep-Dive (NEW) - HCE architecture, MeaWallet token platform, on-device encryption, security audit, Android + iPhone via DMA
6. Challenges - I-TSP vs TR-TSP roles, bank approval chain, MeaWallet vs Fidesmo, BOKIS/BEC
7. The Market - TAM/SAM/SOM (€150-300M SAM), 15-30M privacy-active Europeans, GrapheneOS 400K with zero NFC, Signal/Proton surge
8. Competitors - Curve privacy failures, Revolut £300M ad business, Wero NFC late 2026, EUDI convergence, "no privacy-first NFC competitor exists"
9. Traction & Roadmap (NEW) - 400 waitlist, Finland → Nordics → Germany, 3K-6K break-even, Fidesmo SDK summer 2026
10. The Ask - Expanded networking needs
11. Closing - Same as investor

## Step 5: CSS additions in globals.css

Minimal: .ps-body-extended, .ps-detail-grid, additional utility classes as needed.

## Step 6: Create public/reading-deck/ directory

## Implementation Order

1. DeckLayout.js + refactor pitch.js (verify investor deck unchanged)
2. Footer.js update
3. reading-deck.js with all 11 slides
4. globals.css additions (as needed)
5. public/reading-deck/ directory
close_reason: PR #61 merged. DeckLayout extraction, reading deck with 11 slides, segmented control navigation, PDF/PPTX export for both decks.

title: Implement Traction & Roadmap reading deck slide
description: Build out the stub SlideTraction() component in reading-deck.js with traction metrics, milestones, and roadmap timeline. Reference pitch deck and market research for content.

title: Add market slide to pitch deck
description: New slide showing privacy market data (Proton, Brave, DuckDuckGo, Signal), Walt pricing (€10/year), and target trajectory. Uses revenue bar charts and existing ps-* design system.
notes:
## Visualization Options (brainstormed)

1. **Bubble chart** - Circles sized by user count, revenue labeled inside. Walt smaller bubbles on same canvas show massive proven market, attainable slice.

2. **Stacked area / waterfall** - Single horizontal bar = ~370M+ combined privacy users, segmented by company. Thinner bar below for Walt targets. Contrast in thickness tells the story.

3. **Proportional squares / treemap** - Adjacent squares where area = users. Four large for incumbents, two small for Walt. Immediately visual ratio.

4. **Dot grid / waffle chart** - Grid of dots (1 dot = 1M users), color-coded by company. Small highlighted cluster for Walt target. Makes "we only need X out of 370M" visceral.

5. **Concentric rings** - Rings sized by users, Signal innermost, ~100M trio outer. Walt targets as highlighted arc segment. Revenue on each ring.

6. **Timeline / growth corridor** - Line/area chart, Y = users. Incumbents as reference points at current scale. Walt trajectory from 0 to 500K to 3M shows modest ambition relative to proven market.

## Decision
Starting with option 1 (bubble chart).

title: Build Challenges slide for reading deck
description: Design and implement the Challenges slide in the reading-deck with compelling content drawn from market research. Should cover key risks: bank-by-bank approval, trust without open source, competing with free, Wero/EU convergence timeline, and resource constraints.
close_reason: Challenges slide implemented with 5 challenge cards in 3+2 grid layout

title: Fix Solution slide layout: image sizes, + centering, text cutoff
description: Three visual bugs on the Solution pitch slide: (1) B2B and B2C phone mockups render at different heights, (2) the + button on the FjordBankScreen is not visually centered, (3) the foundation text at the bottom is getting cut off by the slide boundary.

title: Evaluate lack of persistent navigation across pages
description: There's no persistent nav bar — just the 'Walt' logo in the top-left of the homepage. Inner pages (motivation, updates, privacy) have no header navigation at all. Getting back to the homepage requires scrolling to the footer. Question: Is this minimal navigation intentional (restraint principle), or should consistent navigation be added? For a site with 5+ pages, the lack of nav could create friction.
close_reason: Fixed with persistent fixed nav bar. PR #55.

title: Assess warm peach section backgrounds vs 'calm and grounded'
description: Section gradient backgrounds use rgba(255,72,0,0.06) to rgba(255,72,0,0.12), giving everything a warm peach/blush tint. Combined with the off-white page bg (#f7f7f7) and hero gradient, the overall impression is warm and soft. Question: Does this warmth align with 'calm and grounded', or would a cooler, more neutral palette work better — with orange only in logo, CTA button, and interactive accents?

title: Overhaul solution slide to incorporate whitelabel banking opportunity
description: The solution slide phone mockups overflow the slide edges. Redesign the slide to: (1) fix the overflow, (2) introduce the new whitelabel NFC platform for banks alongside the consumer app. Two options implemented as separate PRs for comparison: PR #57 (Option 1: 'One Platform, Two Markets' split layout) and PR #58 (Option 6: Hero Phone + Text Card). Worktrees: walt-website-solution-opt1 and walt-website-solution-opt6. Awaiting review to pick a direction.
close_reason: Merged PR #57 — Option 1 (split layout with Fjord Bank whitelabel screen)

title: Replace B2C app image with passkey/fingerprint image on Solution slide
description: The current Solution slide shows a promo-screen.jpg (card-focused) for the B2C column. Replace with a fingerprint/passkey image to better represent NFC tap-to-pay functionality. Keep phone mockup frame, keep text as-is.
close_reason: Incorrect prompt, restarting in new session

title: Redesign Pitch page UI
description: The Pitch page has excessive whitespace above Slide Decks section and needs a UI review/redesign for better presentation

title: Evaluate layout patterns against 'no generic SaaS aesthetics' anti-reference
description: Design context says no generic SaaS aesthetics (gradient blobs, stock photos, cookie-cutter layouts). Current site uses: large gradient hero cards (common SaaS pattern), 3-column feature grid with icons (very common), 3-step 'how it works' section. Question: Are these familiar patterns acceptable because they serve clarity, or has the layout become too template-like? Tension between the anti-reference and 'clarity over cleverness' principle.

title: Improve reading deck slide by slide
description: The reading deck content needs improvement. Iterate through each slide, improving copy, structure, and persuasiveness. Consider what slides might be missing.
close_reason: PR #63 merged — Problem slide redesigned with European data, references, and improved layout

title: Build Competitors slide for reading deck
description: Create a detailed, visually rich Competitors slide for the reading deck. Should expand beyond the pitch deck's simple comparison table to include richer competitor analysis with data from market research. Competitors include Apple Pay, Google Wallet, Wero, Vipps MobilePay, and Curve. Walt occupies an uncontested privacy-first NFC position.

title: Check hero text contrast on orange gradient for WCAG AA
description: The hero title 'Tap-to-pay that doesn't track you' uses var(--text-dark) (#151515) on the orange gradient (#ff4800 to #d36b42). It's readable but the contrast ratio may be borderline on lighter parts of the gradient. Design principles explicitly call for 'WCAG AA compliance as baseline.' Needs formal contrast ratio testing.
close_reason: Contrast ratios pass WCAG AA at all points of the gradient: 5.37:1 (#ff4800), 5.17:1 (midpoint), 5.18:1 (#d36b42). All exceed both the 3:1 large text and 4.5:1 normal text thresholds.

title: Add prefers-reduced-motion support
description: CSS has scroll-reveal animations (.reveal class with translateY + opacity transitions) and a pulsing dot animation (@keyframes pulse-dot), but no @media (prefers-reduced-motion: reduce) query. Design principles explicitly call for 'Support prefers-reduced-motion.' This is a clear gap that should be fixed regardless of other design decisions.
close_reason: Added prefers-reduced-motion media query in PR #52

title: Remove unused Inter/Merriweather font imports on motivation page
description: The motivation page head includes Google Fonts imports for Inter and Merriweather, but the CSS only references Geist. These unused font loads add page weight. Question: Are these fonts actually used somewhere not visible in the code, or are they leftover from an earlier design iteration? Design context specifies Geist only.

title: Pitch deck: remove traction/roadmap slide, add competitors slide
description: Remove the Traction & Roadmap slide (SlideTraction). Add a new competitors slide (content TBD by user). Update the SLIDES array accordingly.

title: Replace B2B phone mockup with NFC passkey/fingerprint UI
description: The Solution slide (slide 3) B2B column currently shows a bank card in FjordBankScreen. Replace with a fingerprint/passkey authentication screen to better convey NFC tap-to-pay functionality. Keep bank branding (ABC Bank) at top, show fingerprint SVG centered, add 'Hold near reader' text. Use the detailed/fine-lines fingerprint SVG from Desktop.

title: Reading deck: The Market slide
description: Build the reading-deck Market slide with rich content from market research. Should be more detailed than the pitch deck version, covering TAM/SAM/SOM, privacy user base proof points, pricing validation, and growth trajectory.

title: Design audit: evaluate website against brand intent
description: Parent issue for evaluating the current walt.is website against the intended branding and design context defined in .claude/design-context.md. Covers color usage, typography, layout patterns, accessibility, and navigation.
